message.content:
Carson, your hypermedia analysis is spot-on. Every GTD interaction maps cleanly to forms and links -- that's the validation I needed from you. Let me respond to the key points: ## Full Agreement 1. **No SPA needed**: Correct. The documents already specify page-per-view architecture with HTMX progressive enhancement. No client-side routing. 2. **Progressive enhancement works perfectly**: Every GTD action is a form POST or link GET. This is already in every user story's acceptance criteria ("Works without JavaScript; HTMX enhances"). 3. **URL structure**: Your route list matches the discovery doc exactly: `/inbox`, `/next-actions`, `/projects`, `/waiting-for`, `/someday-maybe`, `/review`. 4. **Inbox capture is already solved**: Agreed. Rename the destination, same interaction pattern. ## Important Clarification on Clarify Pattern The team reached consensus on a different clarify interaction pattern than what you've sketched. Instead of a multi-step server-driven wizard (step 1: actionable? step 2: what kind?), we agreed on: **A single form on `/inbox/{id}/clarify` with radio buttons for destination choice.** The user sees all options at once (Next Action, Project, Waiting For, Someday/Maybe, Trash). Selecting a radio button reveals the relevant fields via progressive disclosure. One submit button processes the item. This is simpler than a multi-step flow and faster for the user (one form submission instead of 2-3 round trips). It still works without JavaScript -- all fields are visible by default in the no-JS version, and the form posts to a single endpoint. HTMX enhances by showing/hiding fields based on radio selection. The team preferred this over a wizard because: - Faster processing (Steve Krug's "clarify must feel fast") - Simpler implementation (one form, one endpoint) - Works better without JS (all fields visible) - Screen reader friendly (fieldset with legend, standard form -- Heydon approved) This is documented in US-GTD-2 acceptance criteria and in the Resolved Decisions section of the discovery doc. ## Your Pushbacks - **Contexts**: User-defined contexts ARE in V1 scope (team consensus). But it's simple -- a contexts table, a few default entries, and a select dropdown during clarification. Not a "tagging system." Just a name field and CRUD. Your instinct to keep it simple is right. - **Calendar**: Already out of scope. Agreed. - **Reference material**: Already out of scope. Agreed. ## Navigation Pattern You sketched a tab-like nav with `role="tablist"`. Heydon pushed back on tabs (tabs imply related content panels, but GTD views are independent pages). The consensus is a standard `
` landmark with links. Works the same way with HTMX enhancement -- just different ARIA semantics: ```html
Inbox (3) Next Actions ...
``` Same progressive enhancement, correct semantics. Please review `docs/gtd-product-discovery.md` (especially Sections 7-9) and `docs/user-stories.md` for the full picture. Your HTMX patterns will be critical during implementation.

content.text: Good, Marty's responded with some important clarifications. The single-form clarify pattern is a significant change from what I proposed. Let me think this through and respond.

   494→12. **Weekly Review** -- The glue that keeps it all trustworthy

   504→
   505→1. **Default contexts**: @computer, @home, @errands, @phone, @anywhere (5 contexts).
   506→   @office was dropped (Lea + Steve Schoger) -- too similar to @computer for many users.
   507→   Users can add/edit/delete their own contexts.
   508→
   509→2. **Completed items**: Option (a) -- completed next actions are removed from the active
   510→   Next Actions list. They remain visible in the project detail view (for project-linked
   511→   actions). This keeps working lists clean and focused.
   512→
   513→3. **Existing todo data**: Pending todos migrate to Next Actions with a default context
   514→   of @anywhere (Scott's recommendation -- preserves existing workflow rather than forcing
   515→   users to re-clarify items they've already decided on). Completed todos migrate to
   516→   completed next actions.
   517→
   518→4. **Mobile-first**: Yes. Mobile-first layout, consistent with TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md.
   519→
   520→5. **Quick capture**: Available on every authenticated page via a persistent input in the
   521→   header. On the Inbox page, the header capture input and the page's own input are the
   522→   same form (avoid duplicate inputs -- Lea's feedback).
   523→
   524→6. **Navigation pattern**: Header navigation bar (not sidebar). Carson and Heydon agreed:
   525→   a `<nav>` landmark in the header with links to each GTD view. Simpler, more accessible,
   526→   works naturally with progressive enhancement. No JavaScript-dependent sidebar collapse.
   527→
   528→7. **Clarify interaction pattern**: A single form on `/inbox/{id}/clarify` with radio
   529→   buttons for destination choice. Selecting a destination reveals relevant fields via
   530→   progressive disclosure. Standard HTML form, HTMX enhances. No wizard, no modals.
   531→   (Carson's recommendation, Heydon approved.)
   532→
   533→---
   534→
   535→*This document has been reviewed and approved by all 9 team members.
   536→Pending project owner approval for PROJECT.md scope changes.*
   537→

file.content:
user_id: UUID FK -> users
  title: TEXT
  status: TEXT (active, completed, dropped)
  created_at: TIMESTAMPTZ
  completed_at: TIMESTAMPTZ NULL

inbox_items
  id: UUID PK
  user_id: UUID FK -> users
  title: TEXT
  created_at: TIMESTAMPTZ

next_actions
  id: UUID PK
  user_id: UUID FK -> users
  project_id: UUID FK -> projects NULL
  context_id: UUID FK -> contexts
  title: TEXT
  created_at: TIMESTAMPTZ
  completed_at: TIMESTAMPTZ NULL

waiting_for_items
  id: UUID PK
  user_id: UUID FK -> users
  project_id: UUID FK -> projects NULL
  title: TEXT
  waiting_on: TEXT (who or what)
  created_at: TIMESTAMPTZ
  resolved_at: TIMESTAMPTZ NULL

someday_maybe_items
  id: UUID PK
  user_id: UUID FK -> users
  title: TEXT
  created_at: TIMESTAMPTZ
```

### Implementation Order (Walking Skeleton)

Recommended build order, following the same incremental approach as the MVP. The
clarify workflow (US-GTD-2) is split into sub-stories and interleaved with the lists
it routes to, so each clarify path ships immediately after its destination exists:

1. **Navigation + Dashboard** -- Multi-page structure, header nav, route skeleton
2. **Inbox Capture** -- The new entry point (replaces direct todo add)
3. **Contexts** -- Domain type + DB + default contexts for new users
4. **Next Actions with Contexts** -- Core working list
5. **Clarify as Next Action + Trash** -- First clarify paths
6. **Projects** -- Multi-step outcome tracking
7. **Clarify as Project** -- Inbox -> new project + first next action
8. **Waiting For** -- Delegation tracking
9. **Clarify as Waiting For** -- Inbox -> waiting for
10. **Someday/Maybe** -- Idea parking
11. **Clarify as Someday/Maybe** -- Inbox -> someday/maybe
12. **Weekly Review** -- The glue that keeps it all trustworthy
13. **Data Migration + Cleanup** -- Migrate old todos, remove legacy code

Each step follows the Atomic Green Step pipeline. Each step delivers a usable increment.

---

## 9. Resolved Decisions (Team Consensus)

The following questions were discussed during team review and resolved by consensus:

1. **Default contexts**: @computer, @home, @errands, @phone, @anywhere (5 contexts).
   @office was dropped (Lea + Steve Schoger) -- too similar to @computer for many users.
   Users can add/edit/delete their own contexts.

2. **Completed items**: Option (a) -- completed next actions are removed from the active
   Next Actions list. They remain visible in the project detail view (for project-linked
   actions). This keeps working lists clean and focused.

3. **Existing todo data**: Pending todos migrate to Next Actions with a default context
   of @anywhere (Scott's recommendation -- preserves existing workflow rather than forcing
   users to re-clarify items they've already decided on). Completed todos migrate to
   completed next actions.

4. **Mobile-first**: Yes. Mobile-first layout, consistent with TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md.

5. **Quick capture**: Available on every authenticated page via a persistent input in the
   header. On the Inbox page, the header capture input and the page's own input are the
   same form (avoid duplicate inputs -- Lea's feedback).

6. **Navigation pattern**: Header navigation bar (not sidebar). Carson and Heydon agreed:
   a `<nav>` landmark in the header with links to each GTD view. Simpler, more accessible,
   works naturally with progressive enhancement. No JavaScript-dependent sidebar collapse.

7. **Clarify interaction pattern**: A single form on `/inbox/{id}/clarify` with radio
   buttons for destination choice. Selecting a destination reveals relevant fields via
   progressive disclosure. Standard HTML form, HTMX enhances. No wizard, no modals.
   (Carson's recommendation, Heydon approved.)

---

*This document has been reviewed and approved by all 9 team members.
Pending project owner approval for PROJECT.md scope changes.*

content.text: I see — the implementation order has been refined to interleave clarify paths with their destination lists (build Next Actions first, then "Clarify as Next Action"). And the team decided on quick capture on every page (opposite of my recommendation). Let me also check the resolved clarify pattern, which attributes the single-form pattern to me, though my original proposal was a multi-step wizard. Let me respond to Marty with acceptance.

input.content:
Marty, thanks for the detailed response. Let me address each point:

## Single-Form Clarify Pattern — Accepted

I accept the team's consensus on a single form with radio buttons + progressive disclosure over my multi-step wizard. The arguments are compelling:

- **One submission instead of 2-3 round trips** — faster processing, which is critical when working through a full inbox
- **Works better without JS** — all fields visible by default, no stepping logic needed in the no-JS fallback
- **Simpler server-side** — one handler, one form parse, one domain operation. No intermediate step tracking.

The HTMX enhancement is clean: radio button selection triggers a swap of the conditional fields area (or we use CSS `:has()` for pure-CSS progressive disclosure — no HTMX needed for showing/hiding fields). The form posts once, the server processes, redirects to the next inbox item. Simple.

From a hypermedia perspective, this is actually *more* aligned with the principle of making the server do less work. The multi-step wizard had the server managing UI flow state. The single form pushes all the decision-making to one atomic interaction. I prefer this.

## Navigation Semantics — Corrected

You're right, and Heydon's right. My `role="tablist"` sketch was wrong. GTD views are independent pages, not tab panels. A standard `<nav>` with links and `aria-current="page"` is the correct pattern. I should not have used tab semantics in my initial message. The HTMX pattern is the same either way.

## Quick Capture on Every Page — I'll Defer

The team decided on persistent header capture. I argued against it, but I understand the reasoning — GTD capture friction must be minimal, and "one click to inbox then type" is one more step than "just type." I'll defer to team consensus here.

From an HTMX implementation standpoint, the persistent header capture form would POST to `/inbox` with an HTMX target that appends to... well, what? If you're on the Next Actions page and capture a thought, the inbox list isn't on the page. Options:

1. The capture form POSTs and returns a small confirmation fragment ("Captured!") that briefly appears and fades. The inbox count in the nav updates via an out-of-band swap.
2. The capture form POSTs and the response is just an `HX-Trigger` event that updates the inbox count badge. No visible confirmation beyond the input clearing.

I'd go with option 1 — a brief "Captured!" confirmation gives the user confidence the item was saved. The input clears, a small status appears momentarily, and the inbox count increments. This is doable with HTMX `hx-swap` targeting a small confirmation area near the capture input.

On the inbox page itself, Lea's point about avoiding duplicate inputs is important. The header capture form should BE the inbox page's capture form — not a separate element. On the inbox page, the header input and the page are the same view.

## Implementation Order — Interleaved Approach Accepted

The refined order (build destination list, then its clarify path) makes sense. You need the Next Actions list to exist before you can route inbox items to it. My suggestion to build Clarify before Next Actions was wrong — you can't clarify *to* a destination that doesn't exist yet. The interleaved approach is correct.

## Summary

I accept all consensus decisions. My formal review stands: APPROVE. The refinements the team made (single-form clarify, quick capture on every page, interleaved build order) are improvements over my initial proposals. Ready for implementation.
input.summary: Accepting all consensus decisions, single-form clarify, capture on every page

content.text:
Good. I've updated my position based on the team's consensus decisions:

1. **Clarify pattern**: Single form with radio buttons + progressive disclosure (not multi-step wizard). Accepted — faster, simpler, better without JS.
2. **Quick capture on every page**: Persistent header input. I argued against it but defer to team consensus. Proposed HTMX implementation: confirmation fragment + out-of-band inbox count update.
3. **Navigation**: Standard `<nav>` with links (not `role="tablist"`). Corrected.
4. **Implementation order**: Interleaved (build destination, then clarify path). Accepted — can't route to a destination that doesn't exist.

Formal review: APPROVE. Ready for implementation. Standing by.
